text:
RESTAGING BONE SCAN AND CT OF THE CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS

CLINICAL INDICATION:
71-year-old male [corrected: 69 years old at time of study] with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer. Known history of metastatic prostate adenocarcinoma (Gleason 9, Grade Group 5) with osseous and pelvic nodal metastases, originally diagnosed approximately two years prior. Patient completed approximately two years of androgen deprivation therapy with leup plus abi acetate/prednisone. Recent biochemical progression confirmed by two consecutive rising PSA values (current PSA 8.7 ng/mL, up from nadir of 1.1 ng/mL) with testosterone maintained at castrate levels (22 ng/dL). Patient reports new onset lumbar back pain over six weeks. abi discontinued. Restaging requested to assess extent of disease and guide next-line therapy.

COMPARISON:
Prior bone scan and CT abdomen/pelvis performed approximately 18 months ago (age 67.5 years), at which time osseous and nodal disease had shown significant interval response to ADT plus Zytiga.

---

TECHNIQUE:

BONE SCAN: Whole-body planar scintigraphy was performed approximately 3 hours following intravenous administration of 25.6 mCi of Tc-99m methylene diphosphonate (MDP). Anterior and posterior whole-body images were obtained. Spot images of the lumbar spine, pelvis, and bilateral lower extremities were obtained for additional characterization.

CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS: Multidetector CT was performed with intravenous contrast (iohexol 300 mg I/mL; volume 100 mL administered at 2.5 mL/sec). Oral contrast was not administered. Helical acquisition with multiplanar reconstructions in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes. Slice thickness 2.5 mm. Note: iodinated contrast volume and rate were adjusted given known chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine 1.4 mg/dL, eGFR ~52; creatinine at time of scan 1.6 mg/dL). Hydration protocol followed per institutional CKD protocol.

---

FINDINGS:

BONE SCAN:

Overall, there is definite worsening of osseous disease compared to the prior study. Whereas the previous examination demonstrated marked improvement in radiotracer uptake at multiple sites consistent with treatment response, the current study shows a clear increase in the number and intensity of lesions throughout the skeleton.

Vertebral column: Persistent, and now more intense, radiotracer uptake at the L2 vertebral body, previously diminished in response to therapy, now comparable in intensity to the diagnostic scan. New moderate-to-intense uptake at the T12 vertebral body, not definitively identified on the prior restaging study. The T9 lesion remains present with activity similar to prior.

Pelvis: The left iliac wing lesion, which had shown marked diminution of uptake on the prior restaging study, now demonstrates recurrent and intensified uptake, confirming reactivation of this site. The right acetabulum lesion similarly shows increased uptake compared to the treatment-response scan. A new focus of moderately increased uptake is identified at the left femoral head, not present on prior imaging.

Femur: The right femoral neck lesion shows increased radiotracer activity compared to the prior restaging study, with the lesion now more conspicuous and photon-dense.

Ribs and thorax: Previously responding lesions at the right 4th rib and the left 6th and 7th ribs show low-level residual uptake, without clear worsening at these sites.

Upper extremity, skull, and sternum: No new foci of abnormal uptake identified in the upper extremities, skull, or sternum at this time.

Summary of osseous disease: Approximately 12 discrete foci of abnormal uptake are identified (increased from 8 at the time of original diagnosis; all previously noted to improve substantially on restaging), reflecting clear-cut worsening compared to the last prior study. No superscan pattern. No evidence of cord compression-level disease on bone scan (clinical correlation advised for new lumbar pain).

---

CT CHEST:

Lungs: The lungs are clear. No focal pulmonary opacities or consolidation. No pulmonary nodules identified. No pleural effusions. No pneumothorax.

Mediastinum and hila: No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes. Heart size is within normal limits. No pericardial effusion. The thoracic aorta is mildly atherosclerotic without aneurysmal dilatation.

Chest wall and soft tissues: No suspicious chest wall lesions. Previously noted right 4th rib lesion and bilateral left rib lesions show cortical irregularity consistent with known osseous metastases, without significant change. No pathologic fractures in the ribs.

---

CT ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size. No focal hepatic lesions identified. No intrahepatic biliary dilation. Portal vein patent.

Biliary system: Gallbladder is present without stones or wall thickening. Common bile duct is not dilated (3 mm).

Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation. No splenic lesions.

Pancreas: Normal in size and parenchymal attenuation. No pancreatic ductal dilation. No peripancreatic stranding.

Adrenal glands: Bilateral adrenal glands appear normal. No adrenal nodules or masses.

Kidneys: Mild bilateral cortical thinning consistent with the patient's known chronic kidney disease. No hydronephrosis. No renal masses or stones. A 4 mm simple-appearing left renal cyst is noted, stable.

Retroperitoneal and mesenteric lymph nodes: The common iliac nodes previously noted to measure up to 1.1 cm (below threshold) now appear slightly more prominent, particularly the right common iliac chain, with a node measuring 1.2 cm in short axis. A new left common iliac lymph node measures 1.2 cm in short axis. These nodes were not clearly enlarged on the prior study and represent interval progression.

Aorta and vasculature: Mild infrarenal atherosclerotic calcification, no aneurysm.

Bowel: Small and large bowel are unremarkable without obstruction or wall thickening.

---

CT PELVIS:

Lymph nodes: Clear interval progression of pelvic lymphadenopathy compared to the prior restaging CT (which had demonstrated significant regression from the diagnostic study). The right obturator lymph node, which had measured 1.8 cm at diagnosis and regressed to 0.8 cm on prior restaging, now measures 1.6 cm in short axis — representing unequivocal re-enlargement and nodal progression. The left external iliac lymph node, which had regressed to 0.6 cm on the prior restaging study, now measures 1.1 cm. A new left common iliac node measuring 1.2 cm is present as described above.

Prostate and seminal vesicles: The prostate region demonstrates post-treatment changes in keeping with ongoing ADT effect. The gland appears mildly reduced in volume compared to the diagnostic CT, though this region is difficult to fully characterize given the predominantly soft tissue changes. No new pelvic masses.

Bladder: Normal bladder wall without thickening or filling defects.

Osseous structures (pelvis, on CT): The left iliac wing demonstrates trabecular coarsening and subtle sclerotic change consistent with reactivation of known osseous metastasis, corresponding to the bone scan finding. The right acetabulum shows mixed lytic-sclerotic changes, slightly more prominent than prior. The right femoral neck shows mild sclerotic change consistent with known osseous disease. No pathologic fracture identified in the acetabulum or femoral neck, though the right femoral neck lesion warrants continued surveillance.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Progressive osseous metastatic disease compared to prior restaging study obtained approximately 18 months ago. The current bone scan now demonstrates approximately 12 lesions (up from 8 at original diagnosis, many of which had shown treatment response on prior restaging). Key sites of worsening include: reactivation of the left iliac wing and right acetabulum, increased activity at the L2 and T12 vertebral bodies, increased activity at the right femoral neck, and a new focus in the left femoral head. The T12 and left femoral head lesions are new compared to prior restaging imaging.

2. Progressive pelvic lymphadenopathy. The right obturator lymph node has re-enlarged to 1.6 cm (from 0.8 cm on prior restaging CT), and the left external iliac node has grown to 1.1 cm. There is a new left common iliac lymph node measuring 1.2 cm.

3. No hepatic, adrenal, or pulmonary metastases identified on this examination.

4. No evidence of pathologic fracture in the right femoral neck, though this site of osseous disease should be followed closely.

5. Mild renal cortical atrophy bilaterally, stable, consistent with known chronic kidney disease.

Overall, the imaging findings are consistent with multifocal disease progression involving the skeleton and pelvic/retroperitoneal lymph nodes in the setting of confirmed biochemical castration-resistant prostate cancer.
